title: Recommended Homebrew
requirements: An SD card, any size up to 32GB
_note: A curated list of the best homebrew apps for the Wii across emulation, media, utilities, and game mods.

steps.title: What homebrew can do
steps.body:
With the Homebrew Channel installed, your Wii can do much more than play retail discs. Homebrew apps let you emulate classic consoles, play media files, manage saves, and run game mods — all from an SD card.

This guide covers the most useful homebrew apps worth putting on your Wii. Each app goes into the `apps` folder on your SD card and appears in the Homebrew Channel.

All apps listed here are free and available from the **Open Shop Channel** (hbb1.oscwii.org).

steps.title: Emulators
steps.body:
The Wii's hardware is powerful enough to emulate several retro consoles accurately:

- **Wii2600** — Atari 2600 emulator. Plays the classic 70s/80s library.
- **FCE Ultra GX** — NES/Famicom emulator. Excellent compatibility, supports USB and SD loading.
- **Snes9x GX** — SNES/Super Famicom emulator. Based on Snes9x, runs most of the library at full speed.
- **Visual Boy Advance GX** — Game Boy Advance emulator. Plays GBA, GB, and GBC games.
- **Genesis Plus GX** — Sega Genesis / Mega Drive, Master System, Game Gear, and Sega CD emulator. Also supports GameCube via the same core.

ROMs go in a `roms` folder on your SD card or USB drive, organized by console name.

steps.title: Media players & file managers
steps.body:
Turn your Wii into a media center:

- **WiiMC** — Media center for the Wii. Plays video files (AVI, MP4, MKV), music, and internet radio. Supports USB and SMB network shares.
- **WiiXplorer** — File explorer with USB, SD, and FTP support. Useful for moving files around on the Wii itself.
- **MPlayer Wii** — Simpler video player, good fallback if WiiMC struggles with a format.

WiiMC is the most actively maintained choice for media playback.

steps.title: Utilities & save managers
steps.body:
Essential tools for managing your Wii:

- **SaveGame Manager GX** — Backup, restore, and transfer Wii save files to/from SD card. Useful for backing up saves before modding games or selling a console.
- **GCMM** — GameCube Memory Card manager. Copies saves between GameCube memory cards and SD card.
- **WiiXplorer** — Also listed in media, but its USB loading and FTP features make it invaluable as a general-purpose utility.

These tools let you preserve save data and manage files without taking the SD card out.

steps.title: Game mods & patchers
steps.body:
Homebrew unlocks custom game content that retail discs can't run:

- **CTGP-R** — Massive Mario Kart Wii mod. Adds over 200 custom tracks, quality-of-life fixes, and online matchmaking via custom servers. Requires a USB drive.
- **New Super Mario Wii mods** — Various mods (Newer Super Mario Bros. Wii, etc.) add new levels, mechanics, and graphics to NSMBW. Loaded via Riivolution.
- **Riivolution** — On-the-fly game patcher. Applies mods to disc-based games without modifying the disc itself. The standard way to run game mods on Wii.

Most mods require the original game disc (or a USB loader with a dump of it).

steps.title: Keep exploring
steps.body:
That covers the essentials, but the homebrew catalog is much bigger. The **Open Shop Channel** (oscwii.org) has a searchable library of every homebrew app for the Wii.

To install any of these apps, download them from the Open Shop Channel, unpack the ZIP into the `apps` folder on your SD card, and launch from the Homebrew Channel. Each app folder must contain a `.dol` file and a `meta.xml` file for the channel to recognize it.

Enjoy your expanded Wii!
